result:
The clock on the microwave blinks 3:47 a.m., a stuttering red pulse that makes the kitchen feel like a heart that forgot how to beat steadily. I am barefoot on cool linoleum, holding a glass of water I do not intend to drink. The ice cubes are already shrinking, surrendering to the temperature of the room, and I think: even the ice is giving up faster than I am.  

Outside, the streetlamp paints the elm leaves silver. They look like antique coins, some lost currency from a country that only exists in dreams. I used to collect foreign coins when I was small, pretended they were keys to invisible cities. I kept them in a velvet Crown Royal bag that smelled faintly of my father’s aftershave. He died before I learned the exchange rate between memory and forgetting.  

The refrigerator hums a minor chord. Appliances at night are more honest; they admit they are keeping things alive—lettuce, yogurt, the leftover lasagna—only to postpone decay. I open the door for the light, not the food. The sudden glare spills across my toes and I see the scar on my left foot, a pale map of a summer I tried to jump a barbed-wire fence on a dare. I was thirteen and believed skin would grow back thicker if I bled dramatically enough. It didn’t. It grew back quieter, like it had learned discretion.  

There is a faint smell of burnt toast haunting the room. Yesterday’s failure still lobbying for attention. I should clean the toaster, but that would mean admitting I care about futures where toast is possible. Instead I trace a finger across the countertop, collecting a thin film of flour and grief. My mother would have wiped in circles, clockwise, as though direction mattered. She believed in small rituals: stirring sauce three times to the left for luck, never placing hats on beds, kissing the outline of plane windows at takeoff. She said it kept the sky from swallow us. She’s in a facility now where the nurses call her “sweetheart” because names take too long to remember. Last week she asked if I was her neighbor’s dog. I said yes, and wagged my tail as best I could.  

I set the untouched water on the table; condensation immediately begins to bloom a ring. That ring is the only immediate thing I’ve accomplished in days. The rest is backlog: unanswered emails, half-read library books, a voicemail from the dentist reminding me that even enamel is temporary. My phone screen lights up with the moon’s reflection—an accidental selfie, my face a bruised watermark. I look like someone who has been waiting for permission to leave a party that ended years ago.  

In the living room, the couch holds the imprint of my body like it’s learning to impersonate me. I lie back into the depression; the cushions exhale. If I stay still long enough, perhaps the fabric will memorize my vertebrae, grow a spine of its own, walk to the store for milk and redemption. Instead, I stare at the ceiling crack that branches like a river delta. I once named its tributaries after ex-lovers: Paul drops south toward the light fixture, Melanie forks west and fades into plaster. The crack never widens, never heals—tectonics on a scale only spiders measure.  

A siren rises somewhere beyond the ridge of rooftops. The sound is so human it feels voyeuristic to listen: a metallic wail that wants to be held. I picture the paramedics inside, gloved hands counting ribs, murmuring stay with us, stay. I wonder what percentage of survival is simply being witnessed. My own pulse answers like a shy knock on a door I’m not ready to open.  

I think about the word emergency. How it contains emerge, a coming forth. How every crisis is also a birth, messy and blood-slick. I was born in the backseat of a Buick, so my mother likes to say I arrived late and inconvenient, already skeptical of arrival gates. Perhaps that’s why I keep living in hallways—rented rooms, unfinished basements, relationships that never quite cross the threshold into certainty.  

The glass of water has now reached room temperature, proof that equilibrium is the universe’s favorite sedative. I drink it anyway, tasting the dust of the cup, the faint metallic apology of pipes. I swallow and feel the cool line descend, a small internal weather system. Somewhere inside me it joins the dark lake that grows an inch each night. I imagine future archeologists drilling core samples of my chest, identifying layers: 80% saline longing, 15% overcaffeinated optimism, 5% cat hair.  

On the windowsill, the orchid my sister gave me is dying with dignified slowness. Its petals are purple bruises, but it still manages to look photogenic, like an aging movie star who refuses to leave the party. I talk to it sometimes, mostly apologies. Plants understand impermanence better than we do; they spend their entire lives practicing letting go—petals, leaves, entire seasons—without ever calling it loss.  

I check the time: 4:12 a.m. The hour when statistics say the most people die in hospitals, when hearts finally tire of gripping. I picture those souls standing at the edge of their bodies, looking back at the monitor’s flatline like it’s a horizon they hadn’t planned to cross. I hope someone holds their hand, whispers a地名 that no longer needs translation.  

My own hands are cold. I press them against my neck, feel the drum of carotid optimism. It’s still there, stubborn metronome. I think of the thousand things I haven’t done: learned to play the theremin, driven to the Pacific just to scatter my father’s last letter, told the barista with the galaxy tattoo that her smile rearranges my bloodstream. The list tastes like tin, but it also tastes like beginning.  

I stand, decide to clean the toaster after all. Turn it upside down over the trash, shake out the black confetti of burnt crumbs. Each fall sounds like applause from a very small, very dark audience. When I plug it back in, the coils glow orange like infant suns. I slice bread, thick and uneven, the way my grandmother did, ignoring the guide grooves. She said perfection was a door that only slammed. The kitchen begins to smell warm, yeasted, possible.  

While it browns, I open the back door. The night air is a wet breath, full of soil and the rumor of rain. Somewhere a raccoon pauses, eyes green coins. We regard each other across the petty threshold of species, both scavengers of the nearly lost. I whisper, go ahead, as if the night belongs to either of us. It scrambles up the fence, tail a question mark disappearing.  

The toaster pops. I butter the slice until it melts into bright lakes. Take a bite; it scalds the roof of my mouth, a small pain I can locate, therefore forgive. I chew slowly, counting thirty-two times like my grandfather taught me, turning catastrophe into pulp that can be swallowed.  

I will go back to bed soon, leave the plate in the sink, a sarcastic moon of butter. I will lie down, fit my body into the cool outline of yesterday’s heat. The blankets will accept me like a returning expedition, slightly damaged, mostly intact. And just before sleep, I will imagine the elm leaves outside trading their silver for ordinary green, the debt of night paid coin by coin. I will picture the ice tray refilling itself, water gathering courage to become solid again. I will forgive the microwave its stutter, the orchid its refusal to bloom, my mother her scenic-route memory.  

Morning is only a few metaphors away. Somewhere already, the bread I ate is turning into the next day’s bones, the burnt crumbs already forgetting they were ever toast.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
